title: White Wine on Cotton
timeEstimate: 5–20 minutes (fresh); aged can develop a yellow ring
stainChemistry: White wine is a much lower-pigment tannin stain than red — whites have a fraction of the anthocyanin load and the acidity is comparable. Fresh white wine on cotton is almost invisible and rinses out with water alone in most cases. The cleanup problem emerges LATER: if a white-wine spill sits overnight, the residual sugar caramelizes + the polyphenols oxidize, producing a faint yellow / tan ring. Cotton tolerates acid-side spotters, NSD, 3% H₂O₂, and hot wash. A common modern cooking use — deglazing with white wine — is the stain's other form and often has added oil.
whyThisWorks: Fresh white wine clears with cold flush alone. Aged white wine needs the full tannin sequence because the sugar + oxidized polyphenol residue behaves like a mild tannin. Cotton handles the chemistry without fuss.
defaultAssumption: Fresh white wine is so easy it often feels like it 'went away' — then the yellow ring shows up after drying if it wasn't properly flushed. Treat even invisible fresh spills with a cold flush + NSD to prevent the aged ring from developing.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Cold water, white cotton terry towel
spottingProtocol.instruction: Cold flush from the BACK of the fabric. Cold only — hot water on white wine's sugar content caramelizes it and locks the residue.
Apply NSD, sponge gently. Handles any residual sugar + polyphenol.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Professional tannin spotter (Chemspec Tannin Spotter or PRENETT-A) — acid-side tannin treatment
spottingProtocol.instruction: Only if aged white-wine ring is present: acid-side spotter by dropper. Fresh stains don't need this step.
For aged yellow ring on white cotton: 3% H₂O₂ on the ring. Skip on dark cotton. One oxidizer at a time.

steps: 1. Cold flush from the back — fresh white wine usually clears here.
3. Aged yellow ring only: acid-side tannin spotter (Chemspec Tannin Spotter / PRENETT-A).
4. Aged ring on whites: 3% H₂O₂ on the shadow.
products: Professional tannin spotter (Chemspec Tannin Spotter or PRENETT-A) — aged only
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold through spotting; hot wash post-chemistry
warnings: White wine sugar caramelizes under heat — cold flush even on seemingly invisible spills prevents an aged ring
Fresh white wine rarely needs more than cold flush + NSD — don't over-treat

homeSolutions.agent: Don't Wait — Cold Flush Even 'Invisible' Spills
homeSolutions.instruction: White wine is tricky because it looks like nothing at first — then, a day later, a yellow ring shows up where the spill was. That yellow ring is sugar caramelization + polyphenol oxidation, and it's harder to remove than the fresh spill would have been. Even if the fabric looks clean, cold-flush the area to prevent the ring.

homeSolutions.agent: Cold Water Flush From the Back
homeSolutions.instruction: Hold the BACK of the stained area under cold running water for 30–60 seconds. The residue — even if invisible — rinses out. Don't use hot water; it caramelizes any sugar.
A drop of Dawn dish soap worked into the spot with a fingertip for a minute. Rinse cool.

If the fabric's been sitting and a yellow ring has developed: mix 1 teaspoon white vinegar with 3 tablespoons cool water, dab on the ring, let sit 3 min, blot. If it's still there on white cotton, dab 3% drugstore hydrogen peroxide for 3–5 min, blot.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix peroxide with chlorine bleach or ammonia. Skip peroxide on dark/dyed cotton.

homeSolutions.agent: Hot Wash, Inspect Before the Dryer
homeSolutions.instruction: Launder on the hottest setting the care label allows. Inspect BEFORE the dryer. If an aged yellow ring is still visible, repeat the vinegar + peroxide pass first.
White wine almost never needs a dry cleaner. Only escalate if the aged ring has been hot-dried and two home rounds didn't clear it.

steps: 1. Cold flush from the BACK of the fabric — even invisible fresh spills, to prevent aged ring.
3. Aged yellow ring: diluted white vinegar + 3% peroxide on whites.
5. Escalate only if aged ring was hot-dried.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if an aged yellow ring has been hot-dried and home round didn't clear it. Rare case.
warnings: Cold water even for 'invisible' fresh spills — prevents aged ring
Hot water caramelizes sugar into a yellow ring
Never mix peroxide with chlorine bleach or ammonia

neverDo: Never skip treating 'invisible' fresh spills — aged ring forms later
Never hot-flush white wine — sugar caramelizes to yellow
fiberSensitivities: Cotton tolerates NSD, acid-side spotters, 3% H₂O₂, hot wash
Dyed cotton: skip or inside-seam-test peroxide

materialWarnings: Treat fresh spills even when invisible — aged ring is the real problem
Peroxide on whites for aged ring

escalation.when: Aged yellow ring has been hot-dried and home round didn't clear it. Rare.
escalation.whatToTell: "White wine on cotton, [fresh / aged yellow ring], home-treated with Dawn + vinegar + [3% H₂O₂ on whites]."

contextQuestions: Fresh spill or aged yellow ring?
Is the cotton white, light, or dark/dyed?
Has it been washed or dryer-cycled since the spill?
Straight wine or cooking-wine (may have added oil)?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: The 'invisible fresh spill → aged yellow ring' trap is the characteristic white-wine failure mode. Even seemingly clean wet spills deserve a cold flush.
